coredata.dc:title: High quantum yield copper nanoclusters integrated with nitrogen-doped carbon dots for off-on ratiometric fluorescence sensing of S2− and Zn2+

coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Jiang Xue
Xiao, Kai
Wu, Xiao Li
Zhong, Zhi Ying
Yuan, Hao Yun
Shen, Shi Gang
Su, Ming
Li, Na
Gao, Zhong Feng
Xia, Fan
coredata.dc:description: Pursuing nanomaterials with high fluorescence quantum yields is of great significance in the fields of bioimaging, medical diagnosis, and food safety monitoring. This work reports on orange-emitting aggregation-induced emission (AIE) copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) integrated with blue-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), which enables highly sensitive detection of S2− and Zn2+ ions through an off-on ratiometric fluorescence method. The highly emissive Cu NCs was doped by Ce3+ with a high quantum yield of 51.30 % in aqueous solution. The S2− can induce fluorescence quenching of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs from orange to blue, while Zn2+ can restore the orange fluorescence. The probe provided linear detection ranges of 0.5–170 μM for S2− and 0.05–200 μM for Zn2+, with detection limits of 0.17 μM and 0.02 μM, respectively. Moreover, a smartphone assistant ratiometric fluorescent test strips were developed for the rapid and visual detection of S2− and Zn2+. The AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs probe exhibited diverse fluorescence color responses, high fluorescence stability, and low cytotoxicity. The ratiometric system was successfully applied to the detection of S2− and Zn2+ in real water samples as well as in cellular and living imaging, demonstrating its potential in biochemical analysis and food safety monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent nanoclusters
Aggregation-induced emission
Ratiometric probe
Ion detection
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity experiments for S2− and Zn2+
Development of paper-based S2− and Zn2+ sensor
Preparation and characterization of AIE Cu NCs
Development of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs ratiometric fluorescence probe
Ratiometric detection of S2− and Zn2+ based on AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs
Sensing mechanism of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs for detecting S2− and Zn2+
Paper-based AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs sensor for detecting S2− and Zn2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 22176080 and 32401191), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grants ZR2023YQ015 and 2024TSGC0824), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Grant B2024201050), Hebei Province Innovation Capability Enhancement Plan Project (Grants 22567620H), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (Grant 20224BAB216116), Innovation Capacity Improvement Plan of Hebei Province High Level Talent Team Construction Project (Grant 225A4802D), Research and Innovation Team of Hebei University (Grant IT2023A03), Natural Science Interdisciplinary Research Program of Hebei University (Grant DXK202307), the Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province (Grant tsqn202312216), and Open Fund of Hebei Province Mineral Resources and Ecological Environment Monitoring Key Laboratory (Grant HBMREEM202303).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The effects of different metal ions on the AIE of Cu NCs. The corresponding fluorescence spectra (b) and photographs (c) under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp illumination. Samples 2 to 26 are Cu NC s with Ce3+, Ln3+, Al3+, Lu3+, Pr3+, Gd3+, Pb2+, Ga3+, Ho3+, Tm3+, Eu3+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Bi3+, K+, Co2+, Cd2+, Na+, Rb+, Cr2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, and Ag+. (d) The fluorescence spectra before and after the introduction of Ce3+.
The fluorescence spectra (a), TEM (b), XPS full spectrum (c), element mapping (d), Cu 2p high-resolution XPS (e), FTIR (f), and zeta potentials (g) of N-CDs, AIE Cu NCs and AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs.

The selectivity of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs for detecting S2− (a) and Zn2+ (c), and photos of the system under daylight (top) and UV lamp (bottom) for S2− (b) and Zn2+ (d). Samples 1 to 40 in (b): Blank, S2−, Zn2+, Mg2+, K+, Cu2+, Cu2+ + EDTA, Fe3+, Fe3+ + EDTA, Na+, Ca2+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, Man, Fru, Glc, Lac, Suc, Val, Gln, Tyr, Met, Asp, Lys, His, Ile, Ala, Try, Phe, Arg, Ser, Asp, Thr, Pro, Glu, Leu, Gly, and Cys. Samples 1 to 40 in (d): Blank, Zn2+, Mg2+, K+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Na+, Ca2+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, Man, Fru, Glc, Lac, Suc, Val, Gln, Tyr, Met, Asp, Lys, His, Ile, Ala, Try, Phe, Arg, Ser, Asp, Thr, Pro, Glu, Leu, Gly, and Cys. The 3D fluorescence spectrum (e), linear regression equation (inset: photos of the system under a UV lamp, f), and CIE chromaticity diagram (g) of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs for S2− detection. The 3D fluorescence spectrum (h), linear regression equation (inset: photos of the system under a UV lamp, i), and CIE chromaticity diagram (j) of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs and S2− for Zn2+ detection. Conditions: AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs (100 μL), Tris-HCl buffer (pH6.8, 100 μL), S2− (800 μL), and Zn2+ (650 μL).

TEM image of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs with S2− (a, scale bar: 500 nm). The fluorescence lifetime of AIE Cu NCs (b) and N-CDs (c) before and after addition of S2−. The influence of temperature on the AIE Cu NCs and S2− system (d). Stern-Volmer plot (e). The zeta potentials of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs with S2− and Zn2+ (f). Element mapping of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs + S2− + Zn2+ system (g, scale bar: 200 nm).

The schematic diagram of the smartphone-assisted platform (a). The linear relationship between B/R and S2− concentration (b). The linear relationship between R/B and Zn2+ concentration (c). Insets of (b) and (c) display photos under daylight and UV lamp. Test strip detection for 0.5–200 μM S2− and 0.05–170 μM Zn2+ (d).

(a) Cell imaging with AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs and sequential detection of S2− and Zn2+. (b–d) The corresponding mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) of cell imaging. (e) Inverted fluorescence microscope images of the stomach and tail of the shrimp. (f) Fluorescence imaging diagram of shrimp with AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs and sequential detection of S2− and Zn2+.
Design principle of highly emissive Cu NCs combined with N-CDs for off-on ratiometric fluorescence sensing of S2− and Zn2+.

head.ce:title: High quantum yield copper nanoclusters integrated with nitrogen-doped carbon dots for off-on ratiometric fluorescence sensing of S2− and Zn2+

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Nano-Geomaterials of Ministry of Education, Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan 430074, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pursuing nanomaterials with high fluorescence quantum yields is of great significance in the fields of bioimaging, medical diagnosis, and food safety monitoring. This work reports on orange-emitting aggregation-induced emission (AIE) copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) integrated with blue-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), which enables highly sensitive detection of S2− and Zn2+ ions through an off-on ratiometric fluorescence method. The highly emissive Cu NCs was doped by Ce3+ with a high quantum yield of 51.30 % in aqueous solution. The S2− can induce fluorescence quenching of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs from orange to blue, while Zn2+ can restore the orange fluorescence. The probe provided linear detection ranges of 0.5–170 μM for S2− and 0.05–200 μM for Zn2+, with detection limits of 0.17 μM and 0.02 μM, respectively. Moreover, a smartphone assistant ratiometric fluorescent test strips were developed for the rapid and visual detection of S2− and Zn2+. The AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs probe exhibited diverse fluorescence color responses, high fluorescence stability, and low cytotoxicity. The ratiometric system was successfully applied to the detection of S2− and Zn2+ in real water samples as well as in cellular and living imaging, demonstrating its potential in biochemical analysis and food safety monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The aggregation-induced emission (AIE) phenomenon has garnered significant attention in the scientific community since its discovery by Tang et al. [1]. AIE-active materials, which exhibit restricted intramolecular motion, have led to the development of AIE-active metal nanoclusters, such as thiolate-protected copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs), as a novel class of metal nanomaterials [2]. These AIE materials are notable for their robust fluorescence in both solid-state and solution phases, coupled with an exceptional resistance to photobleaching, which enhances their utility in fluorescence imaging and sensing applications [3]. Among these, thiolate-protected Cu NCs stand out as ultrasmall nanoparticles (<2 nm) that are stabilized by thiolate ligands [4–6]. The thiolate-protected Cu NCs have attracted considerable interest due to their unique position at the interface between individual atoms and larger nanocrystals, as well as their molecular-like properties, which include fluorescence and quantized charging [7,8]. Particularly, the distinctive fluorescence of Cu NCs, combined with their low toxicity, ultrafine size, and excellent biocompatibility [9], renders them highly suitable for bioimaging and sensing applications. However, the widespread adoption of these nanoclusters in practical applications hinges on the development of synthesis methods that are both general and convenient.

Cu NCs are typically synthesized using a bottom-up approach [10], which involves the reaction of Cu-thiolate complexes with a potent reducing agent such as sodium borohydride [11]. The resultant nanoclusters display luminescence across the spectrum from blue to near-infrared, yet their quantum yield (QY) is generally found to be below 5 %. Alternatively, strongly luminescent Cu-thiolate NCs can be produced through a top-down method by etching Cu nanoparticles (>2 nm) [12]. However, this process is limited by slow decomposition kinetics, often necessitating extended time for reaction completion. Additionally, the method requires laborious post-treatment to isolate the nanoclusters from partially decomposed intermediates. Hence, it is imperative to pursue further investigative efforts to develop straightforward and accessible method for the synthesis of Cu NCs with elevated QY. The synthesis of Cu NCs with enhanced fluorescence QY is essential, as these properties are paramount for fabricating sensors with heightened sensitivity in detecting analytical targets.

Nowadays, ensuring food safety and conducting effective surveillance have become paramount issues that are crucial for safeguarding human health and preserving life safety. Sulfide ions (S2−) are associated with food spoilage through their conversion to hydrogen sulfide (H2S) and other sulfide compounds under physiological pH conditions [13,14]. Excessive levels of sulfide can lead to serious health problems such as dizziness, liver cirrhosis, Alzheimer's, and Down syndrome [15,16]. Moreover, the presence of sulfide in food can also be a result of the illegal use of sulfur-containing bleaching agents and preservatives, which poses a challenge for food safety. Zinc ions (Zn2+), serving as essential trace elements in the context of food additives, play a critical role in the modulation of inflammatory responses, the maintenance of glucose homeostasis, and the preservation of cognitive functions [17]. The homeostatic balance of Zn2+ is imperative for overall health, as both deficiencies and surpluses can precipitate detrimental health outcomes [18]. Many analytical techniques have been documented for detecting S2− and Zn2+, mainly including atomic absorption spectroscopy [19,20], colorimetric methods [21], and fluorescence spectroscopy [22,23]. Among these analytical techniques, atomic absorption spectroscopy necessitates the use of bulky instrumentation and requires the replacement of element-specific lamps for different analyses, rendering the process intricate and precluding its application for on-site, visual detection. While colorimetric methods offer simplicity in operation, their detection outcomes are susceptible to interference from colored substances within samples, thereby compromising the accuracy of the measurements. In contrast, fluorescence spectroscopy distinguishes itself by enabling visually intuitive and rapid detection. Ratiometric fluorescence sensing technology excels by inherently correcting for interferences from other substances, thereby enhancing detection precision. Consequently, ratiometric fluorescent sensors hold significant potential for application in the realm of on-site, visual ion detection. Moreover, in recent studies, fluorescent sensors for detecting ions have commonly utilized organic dyes, which can introduce significant toxicity issues during analytical procedures [24], which has led to a growing preference for employing less toxic probes as alternatives. Cu NCs have emerged as promising candidates for the detection of ions because of their negligible toxicity and enhanced fluorescence. Moreover, compared to conventional fluorescence analysis methods, ratiometric fluorescence sensing offers the advantage of dual-emission fluorophores that effectively mitigate errors arising from receptor concentration, environmental factors, and instrumental variations, thereby enhancing the precision and reliability of sensing detection [25]. However, the sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescence approach is often constrained by the limited QY of the fluorescent probes utilized.

Herein, this study presents the development of an AIE-active copper nanocluster system with orange fluorescence, coupled with blue-emitting N-CDs, for the highly sensitive and ratiometric fluorescence detection of S2− and Zn2+ ions (Scheme 1 ). The 10-fold fluorescence QY enhancement (51.30 %) of Cu NCs were achieved with the introduction of Ce3+. Employing the AIE-active Cu NCs, we have engineered a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe by integrating N-CDs. The orange fluorescence of the AIE Cu NCs is selectively quenched by S2− and subsequently restored by the presence of Zn2+, while the blue fluorescence of the N-CDs, serving as an internal reference, exhibits minimal variation. The developed AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs have been further employed to fabricate a smartphone-based sensing platform utilizing a fluorescent test strip, which facilitate the rapid and visual detection of S2− and Zn2+. The ratiometric sensing platform has been effectively applied to fluorescence imaging in A549 lung cancer cells and living shrimp, extending its potential use to food safety monitoring and health monitoring.

Anhydrous copper sulfate (3 mL, 10 mM) was added to ultrapure water (2.4 mL) with stirring at room temperature. Subsequently, GSH (1.2 mL, 0.2 M) was slowly added to the above solution. The solution gradually turned to gray-black to a light yellow, and finally the solution gradually became white turbid, followed by heating at 37 °C for 30 min. Finally, NaOH solution (1 M) was added until the mixture gradually became transparent and light yellow. Finally, the reaction solution was stirred vigorously and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h to obtain Cu NCs. Next, the Ce(NO3)3•6H2O solution (50 μL, 20 mM) was added to the previously prepared Cu NCs solution (400 μL) with stirring at room temperature for 10 min to obtained AIE Cu NCs. The above solution was dialyzed against H2O by a 500 Da dialysis bags for 2 h to remove unreacted ions and GSH, which was then freeze-dried for future use.

The N-CDs (1 mg/mL) were mixed with AIE Cu NCs (0.5 mg/mL) at the volume ratio of 1:3. The fluorescence intensity ratio of N-CDs at 440 nm to that of AIE Cu NCs at 580 nm under excitation of 360 nm is about 1, which indicated the required system AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs was obtained and the corresponding concentration of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs was defined as 1✕ M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity experiments for S2− and Zn2+
ce:section.ce:para: The AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs (100 μL) and Tris-HCl buffer (pH6.8, 100 μL) were added to S2− standard solution at different concentrations (800 μL), and incubated at room temperature for 5 min. The fluorescence spectra of the mixture were measured under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Thereafter, AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs and S2− was regarded as a secondary fluorescence sensor. A series of Zn2+ ions of different concentrations (650 μL) were added to the reaction system of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs and S2− and incubated at room temperature for 10 min. Then, the changes of fluorescence intensity at 440 nm and 580 nm were collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of paper-based S2− and Zn2+ sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The test strips were cut into a specific shape with a hole punch. The test strips were immersed in the solution of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs and AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs-S2- for 1 h, respectively. Then, the test strips were dried naturally in the dark environment. Then, the AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs paper were wiped with a disposable cotton swab dipped in different concentrations of S2−. The different concentrations of Zn2+ solution was added to the AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs-S2- paper with a disposable cotton swab. Finally, a smartphone was used to take pictures of these test strips after the reaction, respectively. The corresponding RGB values of the images were recognized by the color picker APP. According to the relationship between RGB value of the image and concentration, we can establish a date analysis model to achieve visual detection of S2− and Zn2+.
Instruments, regents and other experimental details were described in the Supplementary data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of AIE Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu NCs were successfully synthesized using GSH as template and reducing agent by the one pot method. Through optimal experiments, highly fluorescent Cu NCs were obtained with ratio of GSH and CuSO4 (8:1) at pH 4.7 (Figs. S1a and 1b). The fluorescence intensity of Cu NCs was investigated at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S2a), and the maximum excitation wavelength was determined to be 360 nm. The emission spectrum achieved the maximum fluorescence intensity at 605 nm, with a large Stokes shift of 245 nm (Fig. S2b). At the same time, Cu NCs solution appeared light yellow under a daylight lamp and exhibited a weak red fluorescence under a UV lamp (inset of Fig. S2b). The TEM image showed the Cu NCs were monodisperse and spherical with average particle size of 2 nm (Fig. S3). As the FTIR absorption peak of –SH in GSH was at 2525 cm−1, the FTIR peak of –SH in Cu NCs disappeared at 2520 cm−1, indicating that Cu–S bond was formed (Fig. S4). These results suggest the successful synthesis of Cu NCs.

When exploring the fluorescence of Cu NCs with addition of different metal ions, we surprisedly found that Ce3+ could induce the AIE of Cu NCs. Thereafter, the fluorescence properties of Cu NCs were systematically studied induced by metal ions (Fig. 1 ). As it can be seen, some metal ions (Ce3+, In3+, Al3+, Lu3+, Pr3+, Gd3+, Pb2+, Ga3+, Ho3+, and Tm3+) could significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity of Cu NCs, and Ce3+ exhibited the most obvious enhancement effect (Fig. 1a–c). This may be attributed to the presence of strong coordination bonds on the surface of Cu NCs and these metal ions [26]. It is inferred that Ce3+ forms coordination bonds with the surrounding GSH, and the movement of GSH molecules is restricted, which can effectively inhibit the non-radiative relaxation process and improve the fluorescence efficiency of Cu NCs [27]. As shown in Fig. 1d, the fluorescence intensity of Cu NCs was about 100 times higher than after adding Ce3+. In addition, the fluorescence QY was enhanced to 51.30 % that was 10 times after introducing Ce3+ (Table S1). Subsequently, the concentration of Cu NCs, the doping of Ce3+, and the reaction duration were meticulously optimized to yield highly fluorescent Cu NCs (Figs. S1c–1e). The resulting AIE Cu NCs demonstrated intense fluorescence at 580 nm upon excitation at 330 nm (Fig. S5). The observed blue shift of 25 nm in the emission wavelength of the Cu NCs could be ascribed to the complexation of Ce3+ with the carboxyl group [28]. The bonding between Ce3+ and the ligand molecules induces the connection and aggregation of nearby Cu NCs. This aggregated structure facilitates ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) and/or ligand-metal-metal charge transfer (LMMCT), leading to radiative transitions of metal-centered triplet states. Moreover, the aggregated structure inhibits molecular rotation and vibration, thereby enhancing the fluorescence of the Cu NCs solution [29]. Under ambient lighting, the Cu NCs solution underwent a transformation from a clear and transparent state to a white suspension (inset of Fig. S5b). In comparison to other nanoclusters, the Ce3+-induced AIE Cu NCs exhibited exceptional fluorescence with a notably higher quantum yield (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The image of TEM and FTIR spectra were investigated to further confirm the formation of AIE Cu NCs (Fig. S6). The AIE phenomenon in Cu NCs became distinctly apparent following the addition of Ce3+ (Fig. S6a). The stretching vibration peak of –OH disappeared in FTIR spectrum, which confirmed coordination of the –COOH groups with Ce3+ (Fig. S6b). The UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu NCs and AIE Cu NCs exhibited two bands at 260 and 325 nm (Fig. S6c), which may be caused by the transition of the Cu–S bond to the Cu–Cu hybrid bond orbitals [30,31]. Cu NCs showed a negative surface charge of −14.03 mV as –COOH groups in GSH. After the addition of Ce3+, the surface charge of AIE Cu NCs was −12.98 mV (Fig. S6d). As a result, it can be proved that Cu NCs was successfully coordinated with Ce3+, producing AIE Cu NCs.

Considering individual fluorescence signal was easily influenced by environmental conditions, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor can self-calibrate and result in more accurate and precise detection results. The carbon dots (CDs) with blue fluorescence can serves as a reference signal for dual-emission ratiometric sensors [32]. When coupled with AIE Cu NCs, the blue fluorescent N-CDs can be combined with orange fluorescent AIE Cu NCs to form a dual-emission system. This dual-emission characteristic allows for the monitoring of the presence of target analytes by comparing the ratio of the two emission intensities, thereby enhancing the selectivity, sensitivity, and accuracy of detection system. CDs can be a potential candidate because of their easy preparation, high productivity, and outstanding fluorescence property [33,34]. The nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) were synthesized and characterized (Figs. S7–S11) [35]. The N-CDs exhibited a prominent characteristic absorption peak at 240 nm and 360 nm (Fig. S7) with the fluorescence excitation and emission at 360 nm and 440 nm (Fig. S8). The TEM image of N-CDs displayed that N-CDs were regularly spherical with particle-size distributions from 0.99 to 2.1 nm, and the average diameter was 1.15 nm (Fig. S9). The surface morphology of N-CDs was analyzed with FTIR and XPS, and the results showed that surface of N-CDs were rich in –COOH and –NH2 (Figs. S10 and S11). Thereafter, the prepared N-CDs were added to Cu NCs to build ratiometric fluorescence probes. The ratio of N-CDs to AIE Cu NCs (3:1) were explored to gain proper fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2 a and S1f). TEM analysis (Fig. 2b) reveals that the spherical structures of N-CDs are adsorbed into the framework structure of AIE Cu NCs. The XPS and element mapping (Fig. 2c and d) confirmed the S, C, N, O, Cu, and Ce homo-dispersed on AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs. The characteristic peaks in Cu 2p high-resolution XPS located at 931.7 eV and 951.5 eV were attributed to Cu 2p1 /2 and Cu 2p3 /2, respectively (Fig. 2e), which showed that Cu in AIE Cu NCs mainly exists in the form of Cu (0) or Cu (I) [36]. The FTIR presented that the N–H bond at 752 cm−1 disappeared, but a new characteristic peak at 1540 cm−1 appeared after the addition of N-CDs (Fig. 2f). It is inferred that the –NH2 is reacted with –COOH to generate amide bonds, which was also confirmed by zeta-potential results. AIE Cu NCs had a negative surface charge of −12.98 mV. With the addition of N-CDs, the surface charge shifted to −21.77 mV (Fig. 2g). The results showed that AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs was successfully synthesized. To fit practical application, the stability of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs was demonstrated under different conditions and satisfied results were obtained (Fig. S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of S2− and Zn2+ based on AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To take full advantage of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs to construct highly sensitive analytical system, the responses of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs to common ions and biomolecules were conducted (Fig. 3 a and b). The ratio of fluorescence (F440/F580) of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs was increased obviously after addition of S2−. When different concentrations of anions, cations, amino acids (including cysteine), and sugars were added separately, the fluorescence ratio only changed obviously with S2−. It is concluded that the negative charge of S2− has a higher interaction with AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs than the neutral thiol group. In addition, AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs can be partially quenched by Cu2+ and Fe3+, mainly due to the strong affinity between mercapto groups of AIE Cu NCs and Cu2+ or Fe3+ [37]. The addition of the masking agent EDTA during detection can avoid the interference. The above results indicate that AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs have excellent selectivity for S2−. Through similar exploration, it is found that S2− quenched AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs can be selectively recovered by Zn2+ considering that Zn2+ can form precipitation with S2− (Fig. 3c and d).

Thereafter, the optimal experiments were performed to improve the sensitivity of the AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs ratiometric system, including concentration of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs, pH value, and reaction time (Fig. S13). Under optimal experimental conditions, the sensitivity of the sensor was studied for the detection of S2− and Zn2+. With the increase of S2− added in the AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity at 580 nm was gradually decreased, while the slight decrease in fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was negligible (Fig. 3e). The F440/F580 exhibited linear relationship with 0.5–170 μM S2− (Fig. 3f). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.17 μM based on 3σ/slope (σ is the standard deviation of 12 blank samples). When a series of different concentrations of S2− were added to the solution, the fluorescence of the solution underwent changes from orange to pink to purple to blue under 365 nm (inset of Fig. 3g). The CIE chromaticity diagram of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs showed the trend of fluorescence color change with increasing S2− concentration (Fig. 3h), consistent with the fluorescence photographs under the UV lamp. Subsequently, AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs and S2− were used as a secondary fluorescence sensing platform to evaluate the sensitivity for detecting Zn2+. With the increase in Zn2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 580 nm gradually increased with the recovered orange fluorescence (Fig. 3h). The obtained linear range is 0.05–200 μM with LOD of 0.02 μM (Fig. 3i). The CIE chromaticity diagram was consistent with the images under the UV lamp (Fig. 3j). Due to the reversible signal response behavior of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs ratiometric fluorescent probe towards S2− and Zn2+, the regenerative capabilities of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs was explored. The fluorescence ratio of the system almost unchanged after 7 cycles (Fig. S14). Notably, the developed sensor can satisfy the World Health Organization's drinking water standard (76 μM) and the serum Zn2+ content in the human body (10 μM) [38,39]. Furthermore, the color changes of this detection system under the UV lamp provided possibilities for subsequent applications in paper-based sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs for detecting S2− and Zn2+
ce:section.ce:para: The off-on response mechanisms of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs for S2− and Zn2+ were studied (Fig. 4 ). The aggregated AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs were transformed to a more dispersed morphology after the addition of S2− (Fig. 4a). The AIE Cu NCs is disrupted, but the spherical structure of the N-CDs remains intact, which may be due to the strong binding affinity of S2⁻ with Ce³⁺ of AIE Cu NCs. The UV–vis adsorption spectrum of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs was found almost unchanged after addition of S2⁻, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of AIE Cu NCs by S2⁻ is dynamic quenching (Fig. S15). Additionally, there is no overlap between the fluorescence emission peak of AIE Cu NCs and the UV–vis absorption peak of S2⁻, suggesting the absence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer. The fluorescence lifetime of the sensing system was subsequently discussed. After the addition of S2⁻, the fluorescence lifetime of AIE Cu NCs changed from 4.50 ns to 3.60 ns, while the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs remained essentially unchanged (Fig. 4b and c). The short of fluorescence lifetime of AIE Cu NCs main attributed to less restriction on rotational vibration of redispersed AIE Cu NCs. Therefore, it can be inferred that the fluorescence quenching caused by S2⁻ is a dynamic quenching mechanism.

To further verify the fluorescence quenching mechanism of AIE Cu NCs with S2⁻, the effects of different reaction temperatures (4, 25, and 37 °C) on AIE Cu NCs with S2⁻ were studied. The response of AIE Cu NCs for S2⁻ is temperature-dependent on the temperature (Fig. 4d). Additionally, the Stern-Volmer plot of AIE Cu NCs at different concentrations of S2⁻ at 25 °C was obtained (Fig. 4e). According to Eq. (1) [40], The Ksv value is 2.28 × 10⁻2 M⁻1, and τ₀ is 4.50 ns. Therefore, the calculated Kq is 5.07 × 10⁶ M⁻1 S⁻1, which is significantly lower than the minimum possible value of static quenching (1 × 101 0 M⁻1 S⁻1). In summary, the quenching process is primarily attributed to dynamic quenching.
(1)
F 0
F
‐ 1 =
K sv
[ Q ]
=
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]

In the equation, F and F₀ represent the fluorescence intensities with and without the addition of S2⁻, respectively. Ksv is the quenching constant, which is also the slope of the linear regression equation between F₀/F and the concentration of S2⁻. [Q] represents the concentration of S2⁻, Kq is the quenching rate constant, and τ₀ is the fluorescence lifetime of AIE Cu NCs.

H₂S and HS⁻ are present in the solution (pH = 6.8) as S2⁻ undergoes hydrolysis in solution. Ce³⁺ can effectively remove S2⁻ from the solution as it can react with S2⁻ to form Ce₂S₃, breaking the equilibrium and causing corrosion of AIE Cu NCs.
(2)
Ce 2
S 3
⇌
2 Ce
3 +
+
3 S
2 ‐
K sp
= 6.03 ×
10
‐ 11
(3)
ZnS ⇌
Zn
2 +
+
S
2 ‐
K sp
= 1.6 ×
10
‐ 24

Furthermore, the surface charges of sensing system were analyzed (Fig. 4f). The surface charge of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs changed from −21.77 to −14.53 mV with S2⁻ (−9.05 mV), indicating a potential charge transfer between S2⁻ and AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs. Moreover, after adding Zn2⁺ (+4.4 mV), the surface charge of the system changed to −10.27 mV, suggesting a stronger interaction between Zn2⁺ and S2⁻. When Zn2⁺ was added to AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs and S2⁻ system, the aggregated state of AIE Cu NCs was restored (Fig. 4g). The mapping images also confirmed the binding between S2⁻ and Zn2⁺ (Fig. 4g). As the Ksp of ZnS is significantly lower than that of Ce₂S₃, ZnS precipitates more easily with the gradual addition of Zn2⁺, which is consistent with the mapping results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs sensor for detecting S2− and Zn2+
ce:section.ce:para: As the proposed sensing system could also achieve visual detection of S2− and Zn2+ through significant fluorescence color changes, a smartphone-assistant method was developed for rapid S2− and Zn2+ detection. The smartphone-assisted platform consists of a UV lamp, dark box, cuvette, and smartphone, and photos were taken with AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs after recognizing different concentrations of S2− and Zn2+. The Color Picker APP was used to analyze the RGB values of obtained photos (Fig. 5 a). Based on the data analysis, the intensity ratios of color channels versus the concentration of S2− and Zn2+ were established, respectively (Fig. 5b and c). The B/R value exhibited linearity with 0.5–200 μM S2− with LOD of 0.17 μM (S/N = 3). The linear relationship between R/B and Zn2+ concentration was found in the range of 0.05–170 μM with LOD of 0.02 μM. The corresponding fluorescence color variations with reverse tendency were shown in inset in Fig. 5b and c. To ascertain the reliability of our smartphone-based sensor platform for S2− and Zn2+ detection, we conducted experiments utilizing various smartphone models. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the detection results from these four smartphones was approximately 3.36 % and 4.27 % for S2− and Zn2+ detection, which confirms the satisfactory reproducibility of our smartphone-assisted sensor. To achieve rapid and portable detection, ratiometric fluorescent test strips were prepared. The color changes of the fluorescent test strips were essentially consistent with those of the solution (Fig. 5d). Hence, we have successfully established a smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent test strip sensing platform that enables sensitively and accurately quantitative analysis and data documentation, alongside a simple, rapid, and economical detection method provided by the test strip. Subsequently, the detection performance (response time, LOD, and linear range) of this method were compared with those of reported methods, showcasing advantages over common sensing system (Tables S3 and S4). These results indicate that this method enables on-site and rapid identification and visual detection of S2− and Zn2+ without the need for large and expensive instruments.

To evaluate the practical application of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs in response to S2− and Zn2+, the ability to detect S2− and Zn2+ was explored in environmental water samples (Tables S5 and S6). The recovery rates of S2− and Zn2+ determined by fluorescence method were 92.9%–102.7 % and 92.1%–109.3 %, respectively, with RSD not exceeding 3.1 % and 0.28 %. In summary, the ratiometric probe can tolerate interferences from external factors, making the detection results reliable.

To demonstrate the feasibility of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs for cell imaging, we further applied this strategy to A549 lung cancer cells. Cytotoxicity in lung cancer cells was studied using the MTT method (Fig. S16). The cell viability was slightly decreased with ratiometric fluorescent probe, but cell survival rates still remained above 80 %, indicating good biocompatibility of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs in lung cancer cells. Under a laser confocal microscope, the imaging of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs with endogenous S2− and Zn2+ were investigated (Fig. 6 a–d). Cells treated with AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence in the blue channel and orange fluorescence in the orange channel. After incubating with S2−, the fluorescence in the blue channel remained essentially unchanged, while the orange channel fluorescence significantly weakened. When lung cancer cells treated with AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs and S2− were further treated with Zn2+, the intracellular orange fluorescence gradually recovered. These results indicate that the ratiometric fluorescent probe can be used to monitor intracellular endogenous S2− and Zn2+.

Additionally, shrimp were selected as an in vivo fluorescence imaging model. The shrimp were co-incubated with AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs for a duration of 24 h. To mitigate background interference from proteins and amino acids present within the shrimp, fluorescence imaging was conducted on discrete parts of the shrimp's body—specifically, the stomach and tail—where endogenous fluorescence interference from the shrimp could be ignored. The red, green, and blue fluorescence images were obtained using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Fig. 6e). Under illumination from a 365 nm UV lamp, a distinct orange fluorescence was observable within the shrimp's tissues following ingestion of AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs (Fig. 6f). However, this orange fluorescence was quenched upon treatment with S2−. Notably, the orange fluorescence in the shrimp was restored after subsequent incubation with Zn2+. Therefore, AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs exhibit potential as ratiometric fluorescent probes for in vivo fluorescence imaging analysis.

In conclusion, we have developed an AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs ratiometric fluorescence strategy that capitalizes on the high quantum yield (51.30 % in aqueous solution) of Ce3+-induced AIE Cu NCs. The synthesized AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs ratiometric fluorescent probes have demonstrated high sensitivity, selectivity, and renewability in detecting S2− and Zn2+, with low limits of detection (0.17 μM for S2− and 0.02 μM for Zn2+) suitable for real applications. The multicolor fluorescence changes are visually discernible under UV lamp, and a smartphone-assisted platform has been established for rapid, simple, and field-deployable analysis and detection of S2− and Zn2+. Furthermore, the AIE Cu NCs/N-CDs system has been effectively utilized for continuous analysis of S2− and Zn2+ in actual water samples. Additionally, this strategy has been successfully applied to fluorescence imaging in cells and shrimp, highlighting its broad potential for intracellular monitoring of S2− and Zn2+. This approach offers a promising foundation for the development of advanced sensing technologies that could revolutionize the detection and monitoring of these critical ions in various biological and food applications.

Jiang Xue Dong: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Kai Xiao: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xiao Li Wu: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Zhi Ying Zhong: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hao Yun Yuan: Investigation, Data curation. Shi Gang Shen: Validation, Resources, Methodology. Ming Su: Visualization, Resources, Formal analysis. Na Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology. Zhong Feng Gao: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Fan Xia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 22176080 and 32401191), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grants ZR2023YQ015 and 2024TSGC0824), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Grant B2024201050), Hebei Province Innovation Capability Enhancement Plan Project (Grants 22567620H), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (Grant 20224BAB216116), Innovation Capacity Improvement Plan of Hebei Province High Level Talent Team Construction Project (Grant 225A4802D), Research and Innovation Team of Hebei University (Grant IT2023A03), Natural Science Interdisciplinary Research Program of Hebei University (Grant DXK202307), the Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province (Grant tsqn202312216), and Open Fund of Hebei Province Mineral Resources and Ecological Environment Monitoring Key Laboratory (Grant HBMREEM202303).
